courses.name: Empire, War, and Revolution in Europe, 1850-1939
courses.description: Examination of Europe from the mid-nineteenth century through the 1930s. Emphasis on how nation-building, empire, war, and revolution transformed Europe’s politics, culture, society, and relationship to the world. Provides context for understanding contemporary Europe. (Field b).

courses.name: Europe in War; Cold War
courses.description: Examination of Europe from the Second World War through the Cold War and beyond. Topics may include wartime occupation regimes and resistance movements; the Holocaust; Cold War divisions and memory cultures; decolonization and migration; youth cultures and protest; and the collapse of communist regimes.

courses.name: History of Northern Canada
courses.description: A historical introduction to northern Canada from pre-contact times to the present. Open only to students in the Nunavut Public Administration certificate program. (Field c).

courses.name: Conflict and Change in Early Canadian History
courses.description: This course explores how colonialism and conflict altered peoples, cultures, and places in what came to be called Canada from pre-contact to the first age of industrialization. Course covers subjects including imperialism, Indigenous-settler relations, slavery, migration, and government, providing context for contemporary issues.(Field c).

courses.name: Rethinking Modern Canadian History
courses.description: This course explores how major political, economic, legal, social, and cultural changes shaped modern-day Canada from the late 1800s to the present. It provides context for contemporary issues, including colonialism, redress, reconciliation, race relations, migration and urbanization, globalization, technology, and the environment. (Field c).

courses.name: History of the Global South, 1400-1850
courses.description: This course follows the global community from 1400 to the mid-nineteenth century exploring how global connections, movements and trends have shaped our world. Emphasis on the non-western world. (Field a or d).

courses.name: History of the Global South, 1850 to the present
courses.description: This course follows the global community from the mid-nineteenth century to the present exploring how global connections, movements and trends have shaped our world. Emphasis on the non-western world. (Field a or d).

courses.name: Topics in History
courses.description: A lecture course on a special topic, theme, or period. Topic varies from year to year. (Field will depend on topic).

courses.name: History of Sport
courses.description: This course critically analyzes the evolution of sport from antiquity to the present. The course examines how sport reflects and shapes political and socio-economic processes and what it tells us about class, gender, race, nationalism, imperialism, doping and the cult of celebrity.  (Field e).

courses.name: The Early Medieval World: 300-1000
courses.description: The history of medieval global societies across Europe, Asia and Africa from the fourth to the tenth century as an ‘Age of Experiment’ – fragmenting, transforming and diversifying politics, culture and religion. Students will read a wide range of medieval sources in translation. (Field a).

courses.name: The Late Medieval World: 1000-1500
courses.description: The history of medieval global societies across Europe, Asia and Africa from the eleventh to the sixteenth century as an “Age of Connection” – expanding communication, co-operation and conflict. Students will read a wide range of medieval sources in translation. (Field a).

courses.name: Early Modern Europe 1350-1650
courses.description: A survey of the major social, political and cultural developments in continental Europe from the 14th to the 17th centuries. (Field a).

courses.name: Early Modern Europe 1600-1800
courses.description: A survey of the major social, political and cultural developments in continental Europe during the 17th and 18th centuries. (Field a).

courses.name: Canadian Political History
courses.description: An historical survey of political experiences in Canada. (Field c).

courses.name: Social and Cultural History of Canada
courses.description: A thematic exploration of how the spaces of home, work, and play have been historically produced, understood, and experienced in Canada. (Field c).

courses.name: Colonial Latin America
courses.description: From ancient civilizations to the era of Independence, this class follows conquest, colonization and development of national identity in the countries of Latin America. (Field d).

courses.name: Modern Latin America
courses.description: From the Wars of Independence until the end of the twentieth century, this class follows the emergence of Latin American nations, their economies, politics, culture and international relations. (Field d).

courses.name: Environmental History of Canada
courses.description: A survey of Canadian history considering nature, landscape and geography. Topics include the history of energy regimes and climate change; Indigenous ecological knowledge; colonization and settlement; resource extraction; commodity production; environmental policies and movements.(Field c or e).

courses.name: History of the Indian Ocean World
courses.description: The Indian Ocean is one of the oldest maritime highways in the history of humanity and also an epicentre of global economy in the pre-modern world. The aim of the course is to familiarize students with the non-Western antecedents of modern global history. (Field d).

courses.name: History of the United States to 1865
courses.description: A survey of United States politics and society from the American Revolution to the Civil War. (Field c).

courses.name: History of the United States from 1865
courses.description: A survey of United States politics and society from Reconstruction to the era of globalization. (Field c).

courses.name: Early Modern Britain
courses.description: A survey of significant political and social developments in Britain from the 15th  to the 17th  century. (Field a).

courses.name: Modern Britain & Empire Before 1914
courses.description: A survey of significant political, social, economic, and cultural developments in Britain and its empire in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries. (Field b).

courses.name: Introduction to Women's and Gender History
courses.description: An introductory study of women's and gender history. Themes may include sexuality, masculinity, women's activism, consumer culture, religion, and reproductive rights. Geographic and temporal focus varies from year to year. (Field e).

courses.name: War, Politics, and Society in Twentieth-Century Global France
courses.description: A study of France in global context from the late 19th century to the present. Topics include the First and Second World Wars, colonialism and decolonization, the Algerian War, youth culture and protest, and memory and commemoration. (Field b).

courses.name: 19th-Century Germany
courses.description: The social, cultural, and political history and impact of German nationhood. Topics include the rise of social democracy and the feminist movements, alliance and empire building, scientific racism, sexology, and the emancipation and assimilation of German Jews into the body politic. (Field b).

courses.name: 20th-Century Germany
courses.description: A survey of social, cultural, and political tensions and developments in Germany from World War One to the Fall of the Berlin Wall. (Field b).

courses.name: Modern Britain & Empire, 1914-present
courses.description: A survey of significant political, social, economic, and cultural developments in Britain and empire through the long twentieth century, including decolonization, devolution, and Brexit. (Field b).

courses.name: Ancient and Pre-Colonial Africa
courses.description: Ancient African cultures and civilizations, the trans-Saharan trade system, and the trans-Atlantic and Indian Ocean slave trades from 600 BCE to the 19th century. (Field d).

courses.name: Modern Africa
courses.description: The conquest and colonization of African polities by the European imperial powers from the late 19th century, the 20th century wars of decolonization, and the emergence of independent African nations, including their economies, politics, and culture. (Field d).

courses.name: Introduction to Caribbean History
courses.description: Introduction to the history of the Caribbean that examines the indigenous populations, the role of colonialism and slavery in the construction of plantation societies, the impact of emancipation, and the social, cultural, economic, and political dynamics of the Caribbean in the post-emancipation period. (Field d).

courses.name: War and Society
courses.description: A thematic study of the experience of war and its consequences. Time period, region of the world, and thematic focus to be studied will vary. (field e).

courses.name: History of Japan
courses.description: A survey of Japanese history from the legendary beginning of the country in 660 B.C. to the end of World War Two. (Field a or d).

courses.name: The Historian's Craft
courses.description: Lectures and workshops on historical methods and materials. Topics will include the discovery, evaluation, use and analysis of documents in historical context, non-documentary evidence, statistics, and bibliographical tools.

courses.name: Public History from Memory to Museums
courses.description: Historical representation in the public arena and public engagement with the past, including archives, museums, films, novels, and video games. This course will involve online work, collaborative projects, and field trips. (Field e).

courses.name: Special Subject in Public History
courses.description: A lecture course on a special topic, theme, or period in public history. Topic varies from year to year. (Field e).

courses.name: Origins of the Greeks
courses.description: The history of ancient Greece from the Bronze Age through the Archaic period. (Field a).

courses.name: Democracy to Alexander
courses.description: The history of ancient Greece from the classical period to Alexander. (Field a).

courses.name: Rise of the Roman Empire
courses.description: The history of ancient Rome from early Rome to the end of the Republic (Field a).

courses.name: Rome of the Caesars
courses.description: The history of ancient Rome from the end of the Republic to the coming of Islam. (Field a).

courses.name: Kyivan Rus’ & the Russian Empire to 1801
courses.description: An introduction to the medieval and early modern East Slavic world, including the city-states of Kyivan Rus’, the Mongol conquests, the rise of Moscow, village life and serfdom, and Russia’s 18th century “Great” monarchs. Emphasis on emerging autocracy and lives of ordinary people.

courses.name: Life in Imperial Russia, 1801-1917
courses.description: An introduction to the Russian empire in the 19th century, with an emphasis on how ordinary people built their lives and communities amid political repression. Topics include life in the cities and villages, labour and enslavement (serfdom), resistance, religion, gender roles, education, migration, and more.

courses.name: Special Subject in History
courses.description: A lecture course on a special topic, theme, or period. Topic varies from year to year. (Field will depend on topic).

courses.name: Science and Technology in History
courses.description: Major findings and discussions about the role of science and technology in the past. Topic and time period will vary. (Field a, b, or e).

courses.name: History of Oil
courses.description: Explores the history of oil from the ancient period to the present day. The course uses a transnational approach designed to introduce students to the interconnected histories of oil in countries across the world. (Field e).

courses.name: History of the Modern Middle East
courses.description: This survey begins with the Ottoman Empire and how WWI drew the map of the Middle East. It then analyzes some of the key issues that dominated the region in the 20th century such as Zionism, political Islam, wars, and revolutions, including the Arab Spring.

courses.name: Topics in Ancient History
courses.description: A study of a selected topic in ancient history. (Field a).

courses.name: History at the Movies
courses.description: Considering opportunities offered by historical feature film in the representation of the past, focusing on how historical themes and subjects have been treated in feature films, cinematic uses of the past, the role of film in shaping public memory and understanding the past. (Field e).

courses.name: Medieval Aristocratic Life
courses.description: A general examination of the life of European ruling elites from the ninth to the 13th  century, with special reference to the Anglo-Norman and French experiences of noble  power, conduct, and prestige. (Field a).

courses.name: Medieval Religious Life
courses.description: A general examination of European religious life from the fourth to the fourteenth centuries, with special reference to the cultural and intellectual worlds of medieval monks, nuns, and clerics. (Field a or e).

courses.name: Studies in Greek History
courses.description: Study of a period or theme in Greek History. (Field a).

courses.name: The Later Roman Empire
courses.description: The study of major developments - administrative, ecclesiastical, cultural and societal - of the later Roman Empire. (Field a).

courses.name: Studies in Roman History
courses.description: Study of a period or theme in Roman History. (Field a).

courses.name: Queer(ing) Archives
courses.description: Examination of the archival turn in historical and theoretical perspective with an emphasis on sexuality, race, and gender as subjectivities in queer, trans, and colonial archives. (Field e).

courses.name: Renaissance Europe
courses.description: The political and cultural history of Europe in the fourteenth, fifteenth and sixteenth centuries, with emphasis on the Italian Renaissance and its diffusion into England and France. (Field a).

courses.name: Social History of Sexuality
courses.description: Sexuality in Western society, Middle Ages to the present. Themes include attitudes and behaviour; regulation of sexuality; gender; heterosexuality and homosexuality; prostitution; pornography; the politics of sex: stresses continuities and changes and the understanding of sexuality in contexts of place, class, gender, culture. (Field e).

courses.name: Social History of Alcohol
courses.description: Alcohol in Western society from Ancient times to the present. Production, trade, and consumption of alcohol; religious and social significance; class, gender, and health; drinking cultures; policies toward drunkenness, and alcoholism. Specific topics include comparative trends, temperance movements, and prohibition. (Field e).

courses.name: The Cultural History of Food
courses.description: Food in its agrarian, economic and cultural context from late antiquity to the nineteenth century; production, distribution, and consumption; health, diet and manners; the religious significance of food; food in art; the rise of the restaurant; the birth of gastronomy. (Field e).

courses.name: History of Humanitarian Aid
courses.description: History of humanitarian activities and agencies, both governmental and non-governmental, with particular attention to Canadian involvement. The first half is devoted to early humanitarian traditions, the second to specific agencies such as the Red Cross, Oxfam, Christian Aid, Save the Children and UNICEF. (Field e).

courses.name: Revolution and Society in France, 1789-1799
courses.description: A survey of the French Revolution (1789-99) focusing on attempts to regenerate France and the French through political, economic and cultural reforms. Themes include nationalism, republicanism, violence, legal reform, property redistribution, education, population and family policy, gender, and religion. (Field b).

courses.name: Childhood and Youth in History
courses.description: The role of childhood and youth in modern history.  Topics may include children’s and young people’s relationship to work, education, play, sexuality, the welfare state, war, politics, delinquency, leisure, migrations, and popular culture. (Field e).

courses.name: History of Disability
courses.description: History of disability including the representation and understanding of disability as it changes over time and as it is portrayed and experienced in changing cultural contexts. (Field e).

courses.name: History of the Body
courses.description: The ways in which the human body has been viewed, interpreted, controlled, tended, healed, exercised, measured, pleasured, clothed, and reproduced to create representations of social, political, and cultural relationships. Regions and periods will vary.(Field e).

courses.name: Sports in the Cold War
courses.description: An examination of sport as a way to view Cold War societies and rivalries (1945-1991). Topics include: nationalism, ideology, gender, race, class, ableism, sexuality, the Olympics, drug use and bans, boycotts, and the overall stakes of this battle between communist and capitalist worldviews.

courses.name: Antisemitism, Then and Now
courses.description: An examination of the long history of antisemitism to understand how historical forms of antisemitism have endured into the present and evolved over time.  A variety of texts, images, media representations, and oral histories will be explored using methodologies from history and religious studies.

courses.name: Canadian Business History
courses.description: The place of business in Canadian society, economics and politics. The internal dynamics of Canadian business (organization, strategy, the rise of the manager), and its external implications (competition, foreign investment, business-government relations). (Field c).

courses.name: Place and Politics in Canadian History
courses.description: An exploration of selected topics in the history of one of Canada's regions. Topic varies from year to year. (Field c).

courses.name: Histories of Shopping
courses.description: A study of how the activity of shopping has been understood, practiced, and represented.  Regions and periods will vary. Topics may include: consumerism, service industries, fashion, credit, commodity trades, advertising, department stores, boycotts, shoplifting, and E-Consumerism.

courses.name: Canadian Economic History
courses.description: A survey of Canadian economic history from the sixteenth century to the present. (Field c or e).

courses.name: Selected Topics in Economic History
courses.description: An examination of the economic development of North America or Europe or other possible selected sets of countries. Countries examined vary from year to year. (Field e).

courses.name: Québec Since 1800
courses.description: A social, economic, political, cultural and intellectual history of Québec with emphasis on the development of Québec nationalism. (Field c).

courses.name: Canada-United States Relations
courses.description: An examination of diplomatic, economic, cultural and military relations, with particular attention to the twentieth century. (Field c).

courses.name: Crime and State in History
courses.description: The history of the relationship between the criminal law system and society. Changing issues in the criminal law and the nature of institutional responses, covering medieval to early nineteenth-century England and nineteenth to early twentieth-century Canada. (Field e).

courses.name: Canada's International Policies
courses.description: The development of Canadian attitudes and policies toward international affairs, with emphasis on the 20 th  century. (Field c).

courses.name: Animals in History
courses.description: A historical survey of relations between humans and other animals.  Topics may include history of domestication; hunting; display of animals in zoos, museums and wildlife films; biotechnology; animal welfare movements; companion species; animals as symbols; question of animal agency.  (Field c or e).

courses.name: African-American Women
courses.description: An examination of aspects of the social, cultural, and political history of African-American women since the eighteenth century. (Field c or e).

courses.name: Popular Culture in the U.S.
courses.description: The development of popular culture in the United States. Focusing on a selected theme or time period, the course will examine how popular culture both shaped and reflected broader historical and social developments. Topics may include music, theatre, public entertainments, movies, and television. (Field c).

courses.name: Ideas, Culture, and Society in U.S. History
courses.description: The intellectual, social, and cultural production of the United States, focusing on, among other things, a series of creative tensions: tradition versus modernity; rural versus urban; white versus black; masculine versus feminine; homogenous versus cosmopolitan. (Field c).

courses.name: The United States and Its Borderlands
courses.description: A history of the United States, focusing on the interactions along and across its borders with Mexico, Canada, and the Pacific Rim. This course examines the contests that emerged over colonization, migration, and American state-making. (Field c).

courses.name: The United States in the World
courses.description: The history of the US in a global context. Time period will vary, topics could include world revolutions, imperialism and decolonization, immigration, transnational flows of ideas and people, war, peace, urbanization, capitalism, international law, and the environment. (Field c).

courses.name: Migration and Diaspora in Canada
courses.description: A study of migration and settlement in Canada from the 17th century to the present. (Field c).

courses.name: Women in Canada
courses.description: Selected issues in the history of women in Canada. Themes include women and war, aboriginal women's history, sexuality, the women's movement, immigration, and motherhood. Attention will be paid to the social construction of gender and the intersections of gender with class, ethnicity, race. (Field c).

courses.name: Indigenous Peoples of Canada
courses.description: A survey of indigenous histories in northern North America from earliest times to the present. The course will cover pre-contact histories; military, economic, social, and cultural encounters with newcomers; indigenous experiences with settler colonialism; and the struggle over decolonization. (Field c).

courses.name: Themes in Indigenous History
courses.description: Key themes in the history of North America’s indigenous peoples. Topics may include land and treaties, religious encounters, the law, cultural identity, and transnational indigenous experiences(Field c).

courses.name: Madness in Modern Times
courses.description: History of madness from the eighteenth century to the present. Themes include changing medical understandings and treatments of mental illness, patients' experiences and accounts of psychiatric institutions and treatments, cultural representations of madness in media, and the history of the asylum. (Field e).

courses.name: The Wilsonian Moment: Diplomacy and the Post-Ottoman Middle East
courses.description: This course studies the impact of Woodrow Wilson’s advocacy of self-determination and of the League of Nations on the Post-Ottoman Middle East.  Focusing on particular case studies, the course analyzes characteristics, long-term consequences, and local responses to the Mandate system.

courses.name: History of Modern Egypt
courses.description: Focuses on moments in modern Egyptian history, as exemplified by the lives of particular Egyptians. Through their writings, the course analyses responses to European colonialism, Islamic reformism, Egyptian feminism, the Muslim Brotherhood, Egypt’s cultural influence, the experience of the Coptic community, and the 2011 Revolution.

courses.name: Gender and Sexuality in Modern Europe
courses.description: Exploration of gender, sexuality, and women’s history in Modern Europe. (Field b or e).

courses.name: Aztecs
courses.description: An examination of the Aztec social system, culture, religion, and philosophy both before and after the Spanish conquest. (Field a or d).

courses.name: Reformation Europe
courses.description: A history of the Protestant and Catholic Reformations of the sixteenth century, with special emphasis on the theological disputes of the protagonists and the impact of these disputes on the social, political and cultural developments of the era.  (Field a).

courses.name: Themes in Caribbean History
courses.description: Key themes in the making of the Caribbean. Topics may include slavery and emancipation, Indian and Chinese migration, colonialism, the independence movement, and race relations. (Field d).

courses.name: Mexico: Aztecs to Narcos
courses.description: An examination of the social and cultural history of Mexico from indigenous cultures to the problems of the 20th century. Themes include the continuities of indigenous structures, national identity, wars and political violence, and gender. (Field d).

courses.name: Gender and Sexuality in Latin America
courses.description: An exploration of gender and sexualities in Latin America from the pre-conquest period to the end of the twentieth century. (Field d or e).

courses.name: The Holocaust: Historical and Religious Dimensions
courses.description: Introduction to the historical and religious dimensions of the Holocaust. The foundations, perpetration and consequences of the Nazi Final Solution through primary sources including survivor testimony will be examined. (field b).

courses.name: Themes in South Asian History
courses.description: Key themes in South Asian history. Topics may include the Mughal empire, the British colonial era, the creation and development of states in India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Sri Lanka, and various 20th century historical phenomenon. (Field d).

courses.name: Gender and Sexuality in Africa
courses.description: An exploration of gender and sexualities in Africa from the beginning of colonial rule until the beginning of the 21st century. (Field d or e).

courses.name: The Soviet Union, 1917-1991
courses.description: Explores Russian history before, during, and after the world’s first Marxist revolution, focusing on ideology, society, and control: how ordinary people thrived and suffered in this regime, supported and resisted it, and built their lives and communities amid various degrees of unfreedom.

courses.name: China since the Xinhai [1911] Revolution
courses.description: This class is an intensive introduction to the main political, socio-economic and foreign policy/security aspects of China’s evolution from Republicanism through Maoism to “socialism with Chinese characteristics in the new era,” as represented by Sun Yat-sen/Chiang Kai-shek, Mao Zedong, Deng Xiaoping; Xi Jinping, respectively.

courses.name: Japan Since 1945
courses.description: A political, intellectual and economic history of Japan in the twentieth century, concentrating on the period since the end of the Pacific War. (Field d).

courses.name: Practicum in History
courses.description: An historical research project in a museum or public institution in the Ottawa area conducted under the supervision of the external institution and the History Department. Work includes reading, reports, and meetings. Students should be prepared to devote one day a week to the project.

courses.name: Historical Representations
courses.description: An examination of how historical narratives have been produced in relation to sites of public memory. The public presentation of history through a wide range of themes, which may include museum exhibits, commemorations and popular culture. (Field e).

courses.name: Historical Theory
courses.description: An examination of a wide range of theoretical approaches to history, and a critical reflection on history as a discipline.

courses.name: Digital History
courses.description: The digital representation of history, exploring the approaches, issues, and methods of working in this environment. Topics may include gaming, virtual environments, digital research tools, public digital history. (Field e).

courses.name: Problems in Global and Transnational Histories
courses.description: Historical encounters across geographical regions and ways in which historians studied them. Categories of “national,” “international,” “transnational,” “world,” and “global” history will be evaluated. Themes include: imperialism, postcolonialism, the environment, migration, trade, religion, the body, war, culture, disease. (Field d or e).

courses.name: Crafting Digital History
courses.description: This course applies the creative use of information and media/computing technologies to address the digital cultural heritage issues of public historians, archaeologists, and anthropologists. Topics may include webscraping, data mining, designing and implementing research databases, and visual storytelling of those results. (Field e).

courses.name: Group Practicum
courses.description: A class-based group historical research project done in collaboration with an external institution under the supervision of the institution and the Department. Work includes readings, reports, and meetings. Students should be prepared to devote one full day per week to the project. (Field e).

courses.name: Explorations in Historical Theory
courses.description: Taking a specific historical topic as its focus, this course examines how historians have applied a wide range of theoretical approaches in order to understand and interpret that topic's historical significance. Topics will vary.

courses.name: Topics in European History
courses.description: A lecture course on a special topic in European history. Topic varies from year to year. (Field will depend on topic.).

courses.name: Topics in Canadian History
courses.description: A lecture course on a special topic in Canadian history. Topic varies from year to year.  (Field c).

courses.name: Topics in U.S. History
courses.description: A lecture course on a special topic in United States history. Topic varies from year to year.  (Field c).

courses.name: Topics in International History
courses.description: A lecture course on a special topic in international political or economic history. Topic varies from year to year. (Field b).

courses.name: Topics in World History
courses.description: A lecture course on a special topic in African, Asian, Caribbean, or Latin American history. Topic varies from year to year. (Field d).

courses.name: Transnational Topic
courses.description: A lecture course on a special topic that takes a transnational approach to history. Course content will vary from year to year. (Field e).

courses.name: Thematic Topic
courses.description: A lecture course on a special topic that takes a thematic approach to history. Course content will vary from year to year. (Field e).

courses.name: Topic in Public History
courses.description: A lecture course on a special topic, theme, or period in public history. Topic varies from year to year. (Field e).

courses.name: Seminar in Medieval History
courses.description: An examination of a selected problem in the history of medieval Europe.

courses.name: Medieval History
courses.description: Selected topic in Medieval History. The topic will be specified each year it is offered.

courses.name: Seminar in Early Modern European History
courses.description: A study of a selected problem in the history of Europe during the early modern period.

courses.name: Early Modern European History
courses.description: Selected topic in the history of Europe during the early modern period. The topic will be specified each year it is offered.

courses.name: Seminar in European History
courses.description: Examination of a selected problem or period in the history of Continental Europe.

courses.name: Modern European History
courses.description: Selected topic in the history of Europe. The particular topic will be specified each year it is offered.

courses.name: Topics in Ancient History
courses.description: Intended for Honours students in History and Classics who should normally be in their third- or fourth-year.

courses.name: Canada: Ideas & Culture
courses.description: A seminar on ideas, culture, and society in Canada.

courses.name: Society and Culture in Canada
courses.description: A 0.5 credit seminar course that examines a selected topic on ideas, culture, and society in Canada. The particular topic will be specified each year it is offered.

courses.name: Canada: Politics & Society
courses.description: A seminar on politics and society in Canada.

courses.name: Political History in Canada
courses.description: A 0.5 credit seminar course that examines a selected topic on politics and society in Canada. The particular topic will be specified each year it is offered.

courses.name: Seminar in U.S. History
courses.description: An examination of a selected problem or period in the history of the United States.

courses.name: United States History
courses.description: A 0.5 credit seminar course that examines a selected topic in the history of the United States. The particular topic will be specified each year it is offered.

courses.name: Seminar in British History
courses.description: An explanation of a selected problem or period in the history of Great Britain.

courses.name: British History
courses.description: An explanation of a selected problem or period in the history of Great Britain.

courses.name: Seminar in Women's and Gender History
courses.description: A seminar on the history of women and gender. The particular approach, themes, and historical period will be specified each year.

courses.name: Gender, Sexuality and Women’s History
courses.description: A 0.5 credit seminar course that examines a selected topic on the history of women and gender. The particular topic will be specified each year it is offered.

courses.name: Seminar in Russian History
courses.description: An examination of a selected problem or period in the history of Imperial or post-Imperial Russia.

courses.name: Central Europe, Past and Present
courses.description: Evolution and current status of Central Europe from periods of foreign control in the late nineteenth and twentieth centuries to independent statehood. Particular emphasis will be placed on national accommodations and conflicts.

courses.name: The Balkans in Transition – 1918 to 1989
courses.description: The seminar uses the concept of transition to understand the Balkan encounter with modernity and Europe. Key periods to be examined include the interwar era and the period of communist rule, with an emphasis on political, social and economic themes.

courses.name: Contemporary Europe: From Postwar to the European Union
courses.description: History of contemporary Europe from 1945 to present covering both eastern and western halves of the continent and including social, cultural, political, and economic dimensions.

courses.name: Imperial Russia and the Russian Revolution
courses.description: Examination of the expansion and downfall of tsarist Russia from the eighteenth century to the revolutionary era and the establishment of Bolshevik rule.  Topics include the relationship between the monarchy and subject peoples, social and economic change, and daily life.

courses.name: The Soviet Union
courses.description: Examination of the rise of the Soviet Union to a global power and subsequent tensions that promoted its collapse.  The course will analyze Stalinism, the Second World War, the Thaw, and Brezhnev and Gorbachev eras through the lens of the USSR’s citizens.

courses.name: Seminar in World History
courses.description: An examination of a selected problem or period in the history of Asia, Africa, the Caribbean or Latin America.

courses.name: African History
courses.description: A 0.5 credit seminar course that examines a selected topic in the history of Africa. The particular topic will be specified each year it is offered.

courses.name: South Asian History
courses.description: A 0.5 credit seminar course that examines a selected topic in the history of South Asia. The particular topic will be specified each year it is offered.

courses.name: The Global South
courses.description: A 0.5 credit seminar course that examines a selected topic in the history of the Global South. The particular topic will be specified each year it is offered.

courses.name: Caribbean and Latin American History
courses.description: A 0.5 credit seminar course that examines a selected topic in Caribbean and Latin American history. The particular topic will be specified each year it is offered.

courses.name: Asian History
courses.description: A 0.5 credit seminar course that examines a selected topic in the history of Asia. The particular topic will be specified each year it is offered.

courses.name: Seminar in International History
courses.description: An examination of a selected problem or period in the history of international relations.

courses.name: Seminar on a Transnational or Thematic Topic
courses.description: A seminar on a transnational or thematic topic. The particular topic will be specified each year.

courses.name: Global, Transnational, or Thematic History
courses.description: Selected topic in global and transnational history or on a thematic topic in history. The topic will be specified each year it is offered.

courses.name: Honours Research Project
courses.description: A piece of original historical research conducted independently under the supervision of a faculty member and presented as either a research paper, a documentary film, or a web-based project.  Does not count toward the 4th-year seminar requirement.

courses.name: Topics in History
courses.description: Intended for Honours students in History. Topics will vary from year to year.

courses.name: Topic in Public History
courses.description: Topic varies from year to year.

courses.name: Directed Study
courses.description: Independent study of an historical topic or theme under the supervision of a faculty member. A course outline specifying readings, assignments, and name of faculty member must be submitted to the Undergraduate Supervisor during the first week of the semester.

courses.name: Seminar in Public History
courses.description: Topic varies from year to year.

free_electives.text: 9.  2.0 credits in free electives (may be HIST) 2.0
9.  Sufficient free elective credits to make 20.0 credits for the degree

free_electives.text: 12.  1.0 credit in free electives (may be HIST) 1.0

3.  2.0 credits in free electives (may be HIST) 2.0
